based on empirical evidence, however, for existing research has
yet to systematically examine the news media's presentation of
mass murder.
The Present Study
The present study contributes to the literature by analyzing
newspaper, network television news, and newsweekly magazine
coverage of mass killings that took place between 1976 and 1996.
Supplementary Homicide Reports (SHR) data from 1976 to 1996
(Fox, 1996; Inter-University Consortium for Political and Social
Research, 1998) are used to facilitate the analysis of media coverage because these data contain incident, victim, and offender
information on virtually all of the homicides committed in the
United States during the 21-year period. More specifically, the
SHR are used not only as a source of data on mass killings from
1976 to 1996 but also as a means to locate the incidents that
occurred during this time, considering that victim count, month
and year of occurrence, state in which the homicides took place,
and reporting law enforcement agency are among the variables
recorded.
An attempt was made to locate all newspaper accounts on each
mass murder reported to the SHR by conducting an exhaustive
search of the newspaper databases found in Lexis-Nexis, Dialog@
CARL, and CD Newsbank. A total of 30,027 articles from 117
newspapers were found on 495 massacres that took place during
the 21-year period. The 117 newspapers were published in 41 of
the 50 states, providing a national representation of newspaper
coverage. Multiple regression is used to analyze the extent to
which incident, victim, and offender characteristics affected the
amount of newspaper coverage given to mass killings. Network
television news accounts were found by searching Vanderbilt
University's Television News Archive. The search revealed that
104 incidents were reported by either ABC, NBC, or CBS networks
in their evening newscasts. Newsweekly magazine reports were
found by searching the Reader's Guide to Periodical Literature (1976
to 1998) for the years 1976 to 1998. A total of 23 incidents were
reported by either Time, Newsweek, or U.S. News & World Report.
For both network television news and newsweekly magazine
coverage, multivariate logistic regression is used to analyze the
Duwe / BODY-COUNT JOURNALISM 367
degree to which incident, victim, and offender characteristics
affected the probability that massacres were reported by these
media.
THE MEDIA'S PRESENTATION OF
MASS MURDER: WHY IT MATTERS
The media's portrayal of mass murder warrants close scrutiny
because the public depends heavily on the news media for information about crime. Indeed, Graber (1980, p. 50) found that the
media are regarded as the primary source of information on crime
by 95% of the public. Although the influence of the press is pervasive, this influence declines when alternative sources of information are available, such as knowing someone who has been
accused of a crime, having been victimized personally, or knowing family members, close friends, or acquaintances who have
been victimized (Graber, 1980, p. 51). But the extent to which the
public relies on the media may be greater for mass murder than
for other crimes, considering that mass killings are a relatively
rare occurrence, affecting a small number of people on the whole.
With few if any interpersonal experiences available to most people, the media are the primary and perhaps the only information
source on mass murder for an overwhelming majority of the
public.
Identifying what is newsworthy about mass murder is, therefore, important because the incidents that garner extensive news
coverage are more likely to influence public perceptions and
social policy decisions. High-profile crimes have been the catalyst
for the identification of social problems such as missing children
(Best, 1990), serial murder (Jenkins, 1988, 1994), drug violence
(Chermak, 1997), stalking (Lowney & Best, 1995), and money
laundering (Nichols, 1997). Routine crime stories seldom foster
conditions that are favorable for the social construction of crime
problems because they provide a primarily factual account of
what happened. In contrast, celebrated crimes are reported in great
detail and followed closely over time (Chermak, 1995, pp. 28-41).
Moreover, high-profile cases generate a great deal of interest and
concern, providing reporters and sources (e.g., law enforcement
officials, prosecutors, politicians, academics, interest group
368 HOMICIDE STUDIES / November 2000
activists, and so forth) with an opportunity to make claims about
new or recurring crime problems (Chermak, 1997, p. 705; K. Daly,
1995; Ericson et al., 1987, p. 70; Ericson, Baranek, & Chan, 1989, p.
4; Ericson, Baranek, & Chan, 1991, p. 74; Kappeler, Blumberg, 
Potter, 1996, p. 6). Whereas claims makers use highly publicized
crimes to raise public awareness of a social problem, they also use
these cases to typify the nature of the problem (Chermak, 1997;
Nichols, 1997). In doing so, claims makers shape the perception of
the problem (Best, 1987; Best, 1990, p. 79). And perceptions, as Best
(1995, p. 259), Loseke (1989), and others have pointed out, help
shape the policy recommendations to control a problem.
The importance of high-profile cases in affecting public opinion
and social policy has also been noted in both the academic and
popular literature on mass murder. For example, several scholars
have surmised that public perceptions have been heavily influenced by widely publicized massacres, resulting in a distorted
popular stereotype. Fox and Levin (1998) state that "the widely
held view [is] that the victims of massacres are usually strangers
to their killer who selects them on a random basis after he 'goes
berserk"' (p. 431). Similarly, Petee et al. (1997) specify that "the
public perception of these offenders is generally that of a disgruntled, White male, 30 to 40 years of age, who usually commits suicide at the conclusion of the homicidal episode" (pp. 318-319).
Celebrated mass killings have also been linked to policy debate
concerning workplace violence and gun control. Kelleher (1997)
argues, for example, that Patrick Sherrill's slaughter of 14 people
at an Edmond, Oklahoma, post office in 1986 "marked the first,
widespread recognition of the potential for massive and lethal
violence in the workplace" (p. 169). The workplace massacres carried out by Sherrill and others prompted congressional hearings
on the issue of violence in the U.S. Postal Service (U.S. House of
Representatives Committee on Post Office and Civil Service, 1987,
1992) and helped justify increased government involvement by
agencies such as the National Institute of Occupational Safety and
Health, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and the
Occupational Safety and Health Administration (Kelleher, 1997,
p. 169).
The debate over gun control has long been galvanized by
high-profile massacres. More recently, however, mass shootings
have given rise to concern over assault weapons and large
Duwe / BODY-COUNT JOURNALISM 369
ammunition clips. In 1989, Patrick Purdy used a Model 56S variant of an AK-47 rifle to kill 5 and wound 30 people-most of them
were children-at a schoolyard in Stockton, California. The
author of a 1995 article in the Chicago Tribune (Adams, 1995)
recalled that this incident "packed such emotional power it
ignited the nascent anti-assault weapons movement" (p. M5).
Whereas the Stockton mass shooting initiated the frenzy over
assault weapons and large ammunition clips, the massacres committed by Joseph Wesbecker, George Hennard, Gian Luigi Ferri,
Colin Ferguson, and Dean Mellberg helped give the movement
momentum. In the late 1980s and early 1990s, various assault
weapon bans were passed at the city, county, state, and federal
levels (Tartaro, 1995).
It is beyond the scope of this study to document the social construction of mass murder. However, by delineating the characteristics that make a mass killing more newsworthy, the present
study may shed light on which massacres are more likely to influence public perceptions and social policy. The findings presented
later may also indicate which mass murders are more likely to
engender the copycat or contagion effect. Previous studies have
speculated, for example, that the extensive publicity given to
mass killings encourages individuals predisposed to violence to
commit similar acts of bloodshed (Fox & Levin, 1994, pp. 140-142;
Levin & Fox, 1985, pp. 22-26; Rappaport, 1988). Prior research has
also suggested that the media's coverage of mass killings
increases the public's fear of crime (Fox & Levin, 1994, pp.
139-140; Levin & Fox, 1996). However, the results from this study
are not likely to hold much relevance for this issue because the
fear-of-crime literature indicates that news of distant, random
crimes of violence does not increase the public's fear (Chiricos,
Eschholz, & Gertz, 1997; Heath, 1984; Liska & Baccaglini, 1990).
These findings suggest that although mass killings may increase
the fear of crime locally, they are not likely to affect crime fear from
afar. Thus, massacres that attract widespread news coverage are
not significantly more likely to increase the public's fear of crime
than are those that obtain mostly local coverage.
Although the main focus of this article is the media's presentation of mass murder, the attempt to gather detailed news reports
on each mass killing reported to the SHR contributes to the literature in other ways, as well. A review of the extant research
370 HOMICIDE STUDIES / November 2000
indicates that virtually all of the previous studies of mass killings
suffer from selection bias because they are based on relatively
small samples of the best-known incidents (Dietz, 1986; Hempel,
Meloy, & Richards, 1999; Holmes & Holmes, 1992; Kelleher, 1997;
Palermo, 1997; Palermo & Ross, 1999; Petee et al., 1997;
Rappaport, 1988). The narrow focus on widely publicized cases
stems from the fact that these studies have relied mainly on
national media coverage as a source of data.' As shown later, the
problem with using news accounts as the primary or sole data
source is that the majority of mass killings receive mostly local
coverage. Assembling a relatively large and representative sample of cases is difficult, therefore, unless a substantial number of
geographically dispersed news outlets are used to locate cases of
mass murder.
The SHR generally provide less detailed information than that
offered by news coverage. However, because the SHR contain
information on nearly all of the homicides reported to the police,
they are an invaluable data source on mass murder. Indeed, as the
only researchers to analyze mass killings with SHR data, Fox and
Levin (1994,1998; Levin & Fox, 1996) have made a significant contribution to the literature by using these data to examine the cases
that other studies have failed to take into account, thereby illuminating the "dark figure" of mass murder.2 Although Fox and Levin
(1994; Levin & Fox, 1985) supplemented the analyses of SHR data
in their first two studies by collecting additional information from
various sources (e.g., newspaper accounts, trial transcripts, biographies, and interviews with court and law enforcement personnel), they did so only on a limited basis. Conse- quently, the chief
advantage of the present study over previous research is that it
blends the comprehensiveness of the SHR data with the more
intricate detail provided by news coverage to yield what is arguably the most representative and exhaustive depiction of mass
murder to date. In doing so, this article further contributes to the
literature in the following ways.
First, the present study focuses on an operational issue that has
yet to be adequately addressed by prior research. In using SHR
data to examine mass murder, Fox and Levin (1994, 1998; Levin 
Fox, 1985, 1996) exclude all of the cases in which fire was the
weapon used. The total removal of these cases is not based on a
thorough inspection of the SHR data but rather is predicated on
Duwe / BODY-COUNT JOURNALISM 371
the reasonable assumption that nearly all of the SHR fire-related
cases are not mass murders because the underlying motive is not,
in all probability, death to the victims (Levin & Fox, 1996). Yet, Fox
and Levin (1998; Levin & Fox, 1996) note that the exclusion of
these incidents creates a minor distortion in the analysis of SHR
data because a handful of offenders use fire to commit mass murder. In an effort to minimize the bias produced by the removal of
fire-related incidents, this study attempts to identify which of the
SHR fire-related cases are mass killings on the basis of criteria
established by Fox and Levin (1994, pp. 142-144; Levin & Fox,
1996).
Second, Fox (1996) has noted that the SHR are limited in that
they (a) slightly underreport homicides, (b) sometimes inaccurately record or fail to report data for certain variables, (c) are too
vague in coding the circumstances related to the homicide, and
(d) do not contain important information such as the disposition
of the offender or the location of the offense. By attempting to collect detailed news accounts on each mass killing reported to the
SHR, this study reveals that there are other problems with these
data. The discovery of additional flaws in the SHR is one of modest importance, however, for only 48 cases were affected by
SHR-reporting errors. Nevertheless, the presence of these errors
suggests that researchers should exercise further caution when
using SHR data to analyze mass murder or, for that matter,
homicide.
Finally, this article presents descriptive statistics on a number
of incident, victim, and offender characteristics and compares
these findings with those from previous research. The results presented later regarding the percentage of mass killings involving
assault weapons, suicidal offenders, and offenders who were
motivated by profit are especially noteworthy because the recent
studies that have reported the prevalence of these characteristics
have been based on a subset of mass murders. More specifically,
Roth and Koper (1997, Appendix, p. 7) reported that 4% of the 52
gun-related mass killings from 1992 to 1996 involved assault
weapons, and Petee et al. (1997), who examined 106 publicly
occurring massacres, found that 20% of the 139 offenders in their
sample attempted to commit suicide and that offenders were
motivated by profit (i.e., felony-related murders) in 36% of the
incidents.
372 HOMICIDE STUDIES / November 2000
Perhaps more important, however, the present study reports
the percentage of mass murders in which (a) a disgruntled current
or former employee, client, or customer exacted revenge at the
workplace and (b) the offender killed publicly. Although prior
research has not documented the prevalence of workplace massacres and public locations, both have been thought to figure prominently in mass murder. For example, in discussing the alleged rise
in workplace violence since the mid-1980s, Suplee (1989) proclaimed that "mass murder has seized the workplace with
unprecedented fury" (p. Dl). Moreover, Kelleher (1997) asserts
that the workplace mass killer "has become the most common and
prolific mass murderer in American history" (p. 6). Regarding
location, Palermo (1997) declares that mass murder "generally
takes place in crowded places such as schools, restaurants, and
offices where it is assumed that the number of victims will be
large" (p. 6). Kelleher (1997) echoes this view when he states that
"the mass murderer typically carries out his crime in a public or
semipublic place and in full view of witnesses" (p. 26). The present study attempts to assess the validity of these claims, which are
based not on empirical evidence but rather on speculation.
DEFINING MASS MURDER
According to the literature, it is generally agreed that the definition of mass murder is contingent on the total amount of time over
which the murders take place (to distinguish it from spree and
serial murder) and the number of people killed (Busch & Cavanaugh, 1986; Dietz, 1986; Fox & Levin, 1998; Gresswell & Hollin,
1994; Hempel et al., 1999; Holmes & Holmes, 1992; Levin & Fox,
1985, p. 41; Petee et al., 1997; Ressler, Burgess, & Douglas, 1988,
p. 138). Other criteria have also been mentioned, such as the location of the murders and the geographical distance between murder sites (Holmes & Holmes, 1992), offender motive (Hempel et
al., 1999; Rappaport, 1988), offender age (Hempel et al., 1999), the
type of weapon used (Hempel et al., 1999), the number of
wounded victims (Dietz, 1986), and the number of offenders
(Dietz, 1986). These criteria are not used in the present definition
of mass murder because they are (a) unduly arbitrary or (b) problematic from an operational standpoint. Consistent with prior
Duwe / BODY-COUNT JOURNALISM 373
research (Dietz, 1986; Fox & Levin, 1998; Levin & Fox, 1985, p. 41;
Levin & Fox, 1996; Ressler et al., 1988, p. 138), mass murder is
defined here as an incident in which four or more persons are
murdered within a 24 hour period.'
The SHR and Cases Involving the Use of Fire
Despite having established an operational definition, thereby
demarcating the search parameters for SHR-reported cases, a
review of the literature indicates that the studies that have used
SHR data to examine mass murder-namely, those by Fox and
Levin (1994, 1998; Levin & Fox, 1985, 1996)-have excluded a priori all cases in which fire was the weapon. As noted earlier, however, Fox and Levin (1998) acknowledge that the removal of these
cases may produce a slight bias in the patterns and prevalence of
mass murder because few mass killers specifically select fire as
their weapon. To avoid this bias, an attempt was made to gather
newspaper accounts on the fire-related cases in the SHR to determine which of these cases are mass murders.
Fox and Levin (1994, pp. 142-144; Levin & Fox, 1996) provide
some insight as to which fire-related cases they regard as mass
murders when they cite the incidents involving Julio Gonzalez
and Herminio Elizalde. Gonzalez was responsible for setting the
fire that killed 87 at the Happy Land Social Club in New York City;
as a result, he was later convicted of murder and given a sentence
of 25 years to life. Meanwhile, Elizalde took his life along with
those of his five children when he set fire to their home after dousing his family and later himself with gasoline.
Considering that Fox and Levin have designated these cases as
mass murders, the Gonzalez and Elizalde incidents can be used as
a touchstone by which other fire-related cases may be evaluated.
Therefore, a reasonable criterion for determining whether a firerelated incident is a mass murder is if at least one of the offenders
was convicted of murder (felony murder or murder in the first or
second degree) or was found to have committed suicide. Cases
failing to meet this criterion were dropped from the study. Thus, if
the offender was convicted of either voluntary or involuntary
manslaughter or if the incident was later ruled to be an accident,
the case was not classified as a mass murder. Moreover, cases for
which disposition data could not be obtained were also removed
374 HOMICIDE STUDIES / November 2000
because it could not be established in these situations whether the
offender intended to kill his victims.
The SHR indicated that 183 fire-related cases involving four or
more victims took place between 1976 and 1996. Of these cases, 77
met the criterion for mass murder classification and were accepted
into the study, whereas the remaining 106 were removed.' These
findings indicate that, contrary to the impression by Fox and
Levin (1998; Levin & Fox, 1996), more than just a few mass killers
specifically select fire as a weapon. As a result, the bias produced
by the exclusion of these cases may be greater than they suggest.
For example, the removal of the 77 incidents reduces the prevalence of mass murder by an average of almost four cases per year.
Not surprisingly, omitting these cases has a dramatic impact on
the patterns in weapon use, although, as shown later, it has little
or no effect on the remaining incident, victim, and offender
characteristics.
In their 1998 study in which they examine SHR data from 1976
to 1995, Fox and Levin (1998) report that 78% of the 483 massacres
involved the use of firearms, although they add that this figure is
"slightly elevated" by the removal of fire-related incidents. As
shown in Table 1, however, the exclusion of these cases increases
the level of gun use by at least 10 percentage points.6 Using their
inflated estimate of gun use in mass killings, Fox and Levin (1998)
compare the patterns in weapon use between massacres and singlevictim homicides, noting that mass murderers are more inclined
to use a firearm because it is the most effective means of mass
destruction. The figures presented in Table 1, however, do not
support this conclusion. Rather, even though mass murderers are
more likely to use a gun than other weapons when they kill, they
are not significantly more likely to use a gun than the ordinary
homicide offender. Furthermore, although the proportion of
fire-related mass murders reported here is a conservative estimate, the percentage of massacres in which the weapon was fire is
14 times greater than that seen for ordinary homicide. Thus,
despite the fact that guns are clearly the weapons of choice for
mass murderers, it is also clear that the salient distinction between
mass killings and ordinary homicides in terms of weapon is that
the former are far more likely to involve the use of fire and far less
likely to involve the use of a personal, hands-on type of weapon.

METHOD
The examination of the media's presentation of mass murder
initially involved an attempt to gather newspaper, network television news, and newsweekly magazine reports on massacres that
took place between 1976 and 1996. Newspaper articles were
located by searching the databases found in Lexis-Nexis, Dialog@
CARL, and CD Newsbank. Aside from their availability, these
databases were used primarily because they collectively offer a
relatively large number of news sources, including major newspapers such as The New York Times, newspapers with smaller circulations that are more regional in coverage, and articles from
newswires such as the Associated Press (AP) and United Press
International (UPI). Furthermore, the databases contain newspaper coverage from 1976 to the present, which allows for an examination of cases from the earliest years of the time frame, while also
providing a sufficient amount of time to gather data on the most
recent cases.
Despite the breadth of news coverage, the major limitation of
the databases was that a large majority of the news sources did not
fully cover the 1976 to 1996 time frame. That is, there were often no
data for most of the news sources for the beginning years of this
period. To offset this shortcoming, the indexes for The New York
Times, Chicago Tribune, The Washington Post, The Atlanta JournalConstitution, and Los Angeles Times were searched from 1976 to
1996. Nevertheless, it is likely that there is some degree of error in
measuring newspaper coverage due to the limitations of the
resources used. Results presented later indicate that the measurement error dropped off after 1981, suggesting that the newspaper
coverage was adequate from 1982 to 1996.
376 HOMICIDE STUDIES / November 2000
The SHR revealed that 692 incidents involving four or more victims took place between 1976 and 1996. Despite the fact that each
database differed in terms of how it was organized, the newspaper search for each of the 692 cases used all the sources available in
each database, as indicated by the relatively large number of different newspapers mentioned earlier. The database search initially involved using connected search terms, including the name
of the city or county in which the massacre took place, along with
a descriptive word such as murder, although other terms such as
homicide, shot, shooting, stabbed, slayings, killed, dead,fire, and arson
were also used.' Once a case was located, either the offender's or
victims' names (most often the offender) were then used in a succession of follow-up searches to uncover all additional news
reports that may have been missed by the previous search terms.'
The attempt to maximize the detection of every news report on a
given mass murder was largely successful considering that 30,027
articles were found on the incidents occurring between 1976 and
1996. After the search for each case was completed, the identity of
each news source was recorded for a given case, and the content of
each of the 30,027 articles was examined to record additional data
not provided by the SHR.o
During the search for the 692 cases, 37 mass killings not
reported to the SHR were found and were included for analysis,
whereas 55 SHR-reported cases were removed because they were
not mass murders." Of the 55 cases, 7 were excluded because they
were spree or serial murders. The other 48 cases were removed
due to reporting errors in the SHR, discovered during the newspaper search. Thirty-five cases were excluded because the incident was actually a double homicide that was incorrectly
recorded as a quadruple homicide. In these instances, the victim
count was doubled because the victims were coded twice for the
same incident. Eight cases were excluded because more than one
law enforcement agency reported the same incident. Four cases
were removed because one or more of the recorded victims were
actually wounded nonfatally, whereas one case was excluded
because it was a triple murder-suicide in which the offender was
mistakenly reported as one of the victims. An examination of the
data revealed that the SHR-reporting errors did not have an
appreciable effect on the incident, victim, and offender patterns in
Duwe / BODY-COUNT JOURNALISM 377
mass murder. These errors, however, appear to have a more
noticeable impact on yearly trends in mass murder activity.
After altering the data set according to the corrective actions
mentioned above, the data set showed that newspaper accounts
were found on 495 of the 568 cases (87%). Of the 73 incidents that
were not located through the newspaper search, more than one
third (26) took place between 1976 and 1977, which points to the
inadequacy of the resources used to account for some of the earlier
cases in the 21-year period. It is highly probable that most, if not
all, of the unlocated cases were reported at least locally by the
news media and that the failure to account for these cases stems
from the deficiency of resources mentioned previously. But it is
also possible that at least some were not found due to undetected
reporting errors; that is, they might not have been mass murders.
Despite the fact that newspaper accounts could not be found on 73
of the 568 incidents, the remaining 495 constitute a highly representative and detailed sample of mass murders in light of the high
capture rate (87%) and the rather large number of news reports
coming from a wide variety of newspapers.
Each mass murder located through the search was given a
newspaper score measuring the extent to which it was reported.
The newspaper score derived from a scale developed on the
premise that public perceptions of mass murder are more likely to
be influenced by cases that receive prominent and widespread
news coverage. As such, greater weight was given to articles by
newspapers with larger circulations and to reports by news outlets that were geographically distant from the site where the massacre took place. The reasoning behind placing greater emphasis
on these aspects of news coverage was to offset the bias that might
be produced by simply measuring the newspaper score as the
total number of news reports on a given mass murder. For
instance, under such a scoring system, a mass killing that garnered strictly local yet heavy media coverage might have a newspaper score greater than a more widely reported massacre that
had fewer news reports but a more even distribution of local and
nonlocal coverage. Thus, greater weight was accorded to reports
from news sources that heightened the level of exposure given to
a mass murder.
Each newspaper article on a given mass murder was assigned a
value ranging from 1 to 7, with higher scores indicating greater
378 HOMICIDE STUDIES / November 2000
prominence and visibility of news coverage. The values were
based on both the circulation of the news source and the geographical relationship between the location of the mass killing
and the news source. On the basis of circulation, newspapers were
placed in three categories: gatekeeper, major, and nonmajor. The
New York Times, Chicago Tribune, The Washington Post, Los Angeles
Times, and USA Today were placed in the gatekeeper category
because of their large circulation and their reputation for offering
readers national coverage of the news; together, they provide a
sense of geographic representation.12 Other newspapers were
assigned to the major category if their circulation figures were
among the top 50 daily newspapers according to Editor & Publisher Yearbook (Editor & Publisher 1985-1997). All remaining
newspapers were placed in the nonmajor or regional category.
Greater importance was given to the newspapers in the gatekeeper category because of the large readership and national
prominence associated with these newspapers, whereas newspapers in the nonmajor category were weighted less than those in
the major category because of their smaller circulation.
A news report was considered local if the location of the news
source was within a 100-mile radius from the site where the mass
murder took place. Thus, local news reports generally encompassed incidents occurring within the town, city, county, or metropolitan area of the reporting news source, whereas massacres
occurring outside this territory were regarded as nonlocal.
Greater weight was given to nonlocal news reports because they
tend to increase public awareness of a mass murder more than
articles from local news sources.
A value of 7 was given to articles by any of the newspapers in
the gatekeeper category on nonlocally occurring mass murders.
For example, if The Washington Post reported a mass killing that
occurred in Chicago or Kansas City, the article was given a value
of 7. Values of 6 and 5 were assigned to reports from newspapers
in the major and nonmajor newspapers, respectively, when the
mass murder occurred nonlocally. A value of 4 was given to
reports of locally covered incidents by any of the newspapers in
the gatekeeper category. Values of 3 and 2 were assigned to articles on locally occurring murders from newspapers in the major
and nonmajor categories, respectively. If a massacre took place in
Duwe / BODY-COUNT JOURNALISM 379
Chicago, for example, and was reported by the Chicago Tribune, a
value of 4 was given to the article, whereas a value of 3 was
assigned to an article from The San Diego Union-Tribune on a mass
murder occurring in San Diego. Finally, a value of 1 was given to
news reports from the major newswires found in the Lexis-Nexis
and Dialog@CARL databases. Although this type of source was
often used to account for cases that did not receive newspaper
coverage, the value given to newswire reports was also recorded
for the incidents that were reported by newspapers. A total newspaper score was formed by summing the values of all news
reports on a given mass murder."
Newspaper coverage was analyzed by conducting multiple
regression analyses in which newspaper score was the dependent
variable." The predictors, on the other hand, were 23 incident, victim, and offender variables. These variables, which describe the
mass murder incidents, were derived from both the SHR and
newspaper data. Because newspaper score was an incident measure, the victim and offender variables were converted into incident measures to properly account for these variables in the
regression analyses."
Network television news reports were found by searching
Vanderbilt University's Television News Archive, which has an
electronic database containing transcribed evening newscasts
from the three major television networks: ABC, NBC, and CBS. As
in the newspaper search, television news reports were located by
using a variety of search terms pertaining to characteristics such
as date (e.g., month and year), location (e.g., city or state), and
names of the victims and offenders. A dichotomous variable,
TVNEWS, was created by assigning a value of 1 to the 104 cases
that were presented by ABC, NBC, or CBS networks in their evening newscasts. Massacres not receiving coverage were given a
value of 0. Network television news coverage was examined by
conducting a multivariate logistic regression analysis in which
TVNEWS was the dependent variable and the predictors were the
incident, victim, and offender variables provided by the SHR and
newspaper data.
The analysis of newsweekly magazine coverage began with a
search of the Reader's Guide to Periodical Literature for the years
1976 to 1998. Newsweekly magazine accounts were located by
380 HOMICIDE STUDIES / November 2000
examining the articles listed in the murder category. A dichotomous variable, WEEKLY, was created in which the 23 massacres
reported by either Time, Newsweek, or U.S. News & World Report
were given a value of 1. Incidents not receiving coverage were
assigned a value of 0. To assess newsweekly magazine coverage, a
logistic regression analysis was performed in which WEEKLY
was the dependent variable and the independent variables were
those derived from the SHR and newspaper data.
Descriptive Statistics
Descriptive statistics on the mass killings used in the regression
analyses are presented in Table 2. In describing the patterns of
mass murder, these statistics provide the basis for determining the
extent to which news coverage reflects the social reality of mass
murder."6 They also present a picture of mass murder that is, for
the most part, consistent with prior research. In particular, the figures derived from the SHR data (e.g., victim-offender relationship, region, population group, and victim and offender demographic characteristics) are virtually the same as those reported
by Fox and Levin (1998; Levin & Fox, 1996), indicating that, with
the exception of weapon use, the patterns of mass murder are
largely unaffected by the exclusion of fire-related cases.
As noted earlier, the removal of the fire-related incidents overstates the level of gun use by at least 10 percentage points, because
the present study found that 68% of the mass killings involved the
use of guns, compared with the 78% reported by Fox and Levin
(1998). Although these results indicate little difference between
mass killings and ordinary homicides in terms of the prevalence
of gun use, the larger number of victims in mass murders may be
partly attributable to a greater use of semiautomatic firearms with
high magazine capacities: that is, guns that presumably make it
easier to shoot more people more rapidly. Because assault weapons have semiautomatic capability, can accept large ammunition
clips, and have been used in several high-profile massacres, they
have been referred to as "weapons for crime and mass murder"
("Roberti Joins Lobby," 1994, p. A20) and as "the weapons of
choice of mass murderers" (Ackerman, 1989, p. A7). However,
consistent with prior research (see Roth & Koper, 1997, Appendix,
p. 7), Table 2 shows that assault weapons are used in only 3% of all

only 27% of the incidents involved offenders who killed publicly.

The data revealed that the Oklahoma City and World Trade
Center bombings both had extreme observations for wounded
count (1,042 for World Trade Center and 500 for Oklahoma City)
and newspaper score (16,284 for World Trade Center and 51,073
for Oklahoma City), whereas the death toll for the Oklahoma City

they are essentially unnecessary because the data (a) represent

DISCUSSION
Despite numerous differences in format, there was much agreement among the three news media in terms of which characteristics made a mass killing more newsworthy. Death toll, stranger
victims, and public locations were significant positive predictors
of coverage in all three types of media. Assault weapon use and
workplace massacres were overemphasized by all three news media
and were significant positive predictors of at least two types of
coverage. The results also indicate that interracial massacres and
wounded count had a significant positive effect on newspaper
score and were overrepresented in both newsweekly magazine
and network television coverage. Older offenders-especially
those between the ages of 30 and 49-stimulated greater attention
from the print news media. Finally, guns and other weapons were
significant positive predictors of newspaper coverage, whereas
gun use was overrepresented in newsweekly accounts.
These characteristics enhanced the news value of a mass murder because the news media are compelled to market a crime
product that sells. In pursuit of this objective, news organizations
engage the audience by appealing to their emotions, presenting
informative yet entertaining crime news that depicts crisis and
conflict, tragedy and melodrama (Ericson et al., 1987, p. 15;
Kappeler et al., 1996, p. 6). The emphasis on news as theater is evidenced by the high priority given to violent, interpersonal crimes
such as robbery, assault, and, in particular, homicide. But the news
media place an even greater premium on mass murders because,
as one of the rarest and most extreme forms of violence, these incidents are more likely to seize the public's attention by stirring
their emotions. As noted earlier, almost every mass killing was
locally newsworthy. Not all mass murders, however, were nationally newsworthy, for less than one fourth of the cases captured
extensive newspaper and network television news coverage and
a smaller fraction of incidents attracted attention from all three
news media.
Duwe / BODY-COUNT JOURNALISM 391
The mass murders that garnered greater media exposure were
more newsworthy largely because they were riveting, emotionally evocative incidents that epitomized news as theater-a
morality play involving pure, innocent victims and offenders
who seemingly went "berserk" in a public setting. The strong positive effects that death toll and, to a lesser extent, wounded count
had on all three types of coverage suggest that tragedy figured
prominently in determining the news value of a mass murder.
More specifically, the higher the body count the more newsworthy the mass killing because it was more serious, shocking, and
tragic.
But massacres were even more tragic when strangers were
killed. These incidents conjure up images of random violence
because the slaughter of strangers connotes an indiscriminate
selection of victims. As a result, a sharp distinction is drawn
between victims and offenders: Victims are depicted as blameless
or virtuous, whereas offenders are characterized as evil, crazy,
and less than human. Moreover, the seemingly random selection
of victims broadens the news interest by conveying the impression that anyone could be a victim of a mass killing. In contrast,
massacres involving nonstranger victims had a more limited
news appeal because these incidents tend to be regarded as more
of an intimate matter in which the victims are less likely to be portrayed as innocent or blameless. For example, although family
massacres almost always received local newspaper coverage,
they seldom captured the national spotlight.
Publicly occurring massacres generated a great deal of media
attention because they were sensational, highly visible events that
had widespread significance. These incidents usually involved a
number of people who witnessed and survived the attack, which
gave the news media an invaluable means to deliver a fascinating
firsthand account to the audience, allowing them to vicariously
experience the horror of the event. In addition, the audience is
generally more apt to identify with the victims of these incidents,
for they were killed simply because they were in the wrong place
at the wrong time. Conversely, mass murders in residential settings had less significance as news items because they were private incidents that rarely had any survivors.
The results also indicate that the high-profile mass murders
were more newsworthy because they were more unusual. For
392 HOMICIDE STUDIES / November 2000
example, with the exception of gun use, the characteristics that
heightened the news value of a mass killing were among the least
frequently occurring for mass murder. Furthermore, the findings
regarding workplace massacres and assault weapon use suggest
that novelty also enhanced the news value of a mass murder.
Although these massacres were unusual and often-dramatic displays of violence, their importance as news items may have been
augmented by the perception that emerged in the late 1980s that
workplace violence and assault weapon use constituted new
trends in crime and, more narrowly, mass murder. The extensive
publicity given to these incidents may partly account for the exaggerated claims noted earlier.
CONCLUSION
This study reveals that most mass killings tend to receive local
coverage whereas a small minority garner widespread publicity.
Although the locally reported incidents are more representative
of mass murder, these cases are insulated in that they are generally
known only on a local basis. Conversely, the well-known incidents are the most extreme and atypical instances of mass murder,
which is precisely why they are more newsworthy. To be sure, it is
hardly surprising to find that the news media present a distorted
image of mass murder, for research has consistently shown that
news reporting misrepresents the social reality of crime. Yet, the
overemphasis placed on the rarest and most sensational mass killings is significant because these cases are likely to have a greater
impact on the social construction of mass murder.
High-profile cases appear to play a central role in shaping various perceptions about mass murder. Most notably, the characteristics of the highly publicized massacres are generally consistent
with the popular stereotype described by Fox and Levin (1998)
and Petee et al. (1997). In addition, the media reports examined for
this study indicate that celebrated cases were commonly cited as
typifying examples in a number of feature articles on mass murder. For example, in discussing the topic of mass murder after
George Hennard gunned down 23 at a Luby's Cafeteria in Killeen,
Texas, an article in The Washington Post (Daly, 1991) reported that
"Hennard ... fits what experts say is the classic profile of a mass
Duwe / BODY-COUNT JOURNALISM 393
killer" (p. A13). Similarly, it was stated in a 1993 newspaper article
that "Gian Luigi Ferri was a textbook case of a mass murderer"
(Brazil, Rosenfeld, & Williams, 1993, p. A6) after he killed eight
and wounded six at a law office in San Francisco. Although it is
reasonable to infer that public perceptions have been heavily
influenced by highly publicized incidents, future research should
attempt to clarify this issue by examining the extent to which
claims makers have used celebrated cases to typify mass murder.
Perhaps more important, however, future research should gather
public opinion data to determine whether the public's perception
of mass murder corresponds with the image presented by the
media.
High-profile cases also appear to have a bearing on perceptions
regarding trends in the prevalence of mass murder. For example,
researchers have asserted that the United States has experienced
an unprecedented and ever-growing mass murder wave since the
mid-1960s (Falk, 1990, p. 86; Levin & Fox, 1985, p. 19; Petee et al.,
1997). But this claim is not based on hard data because existing
research has not systematically examined mass murder before
1965. Rather, it is based on the apparent surge in the number of
well-known cases since the mid-1960s. It is debatable, however,
whether high-profile cases are valid indicators of either short- or
long-term trends in mass murder activity because these incidents
make up such a small portion of all mass killings. Despite this contention, it is, nevertheless, clear that historical research needs to be
conducted to accurately determine whether the mid-1960s
marked the beginning of a dramatic increase in the prevalence of
mass murder.
As noted earlier, the policy proposals to control mass murder
have consisted mainly of stronger gun laws, particularly a ban on
assault weapons, and efforts to prevent workplace violence. The
widespread coverage given to massacres involving assault weapons and workplace violence suggests that high-profile cases may
have a greater impact on public policy. Still, the amount of media
coverage alone does not fully explain why mass murder has been
framed as a gun problem, for example, because crime problems
can be framed in a number of ways. As a result, future research
should examine the values and interests of those who have made
claims about mass murder. Attention should also be given to the
role that mass killings play in the creation of crime problems such
394 HOMICIDE STUDIES / November 2000
as assault weapons, workplace violence, or more recently, school
shootings. Exploring this issue may provide a better understanding of the connections often seen among social problems.

**Article Summary:**

**Title:** Unspecified, but involves analysis of media coverage of mass killings

This study examines the media coverage of mass killings reported between 1976 and 1996 in newspapers, network television news, and newsweeklies. It analyzes which mass murders were considered newsworthy on a national level and finds that only a few received such attention, usually based on factors like numerous victims, public locations, use of assault weapons, etc.

**Key Findings:**
- Although most mass murders were reported by local news, a small number became nationally notable.
- Widely publicized mass murders typically involved many fatalities and injuries, stranger victims, public locations, assault weapons, workplace violence, interracial dynamics, and deviations from the typical age range.
- Such incidents are the most extreme and atypical forms of mass murder, matching the public's fascination with rare and sensational violence.

**Discussion:**
- The media's focus on these exceptional events, driven by the aim to maximize profits through audience engagement, may distort the public's perception of the frequency and nature of mass killings.
- High-profile cases contribute to the social construction of mass murder, influencing public opinion and policy debates on issues like workplace violence and gun control.
- Research suggests that media coverage can lead to an overestimation of phenomena like a "mass murder wave" and the prevalence of assault weapons in these crimes.

**Methodology:**
- The study used Supplementary Homicide Reports (SHR) to identify mass killings during the specified period.
- Mass murders were defined as incidents where four or more individuals were killed within 24 hours.
- Coverage was analyzed using multiple databases and reviewing relevant articles to encapsulate the portrayal of mass murder in the media.

**Potential Impacts:**
- The study suggests that the media portrayal of mass murder may influence public opinion and policy, potentially leading to social and legislative changes.
- The skewed media coverage could also contribute to a misunderstanding of trends in violent crime, such as the assumption of increasing mass murder rates since the mid-1960s.

**Conclusion:**
The study emphasized the need to scrutinize media representations of mass murder, understand the influence of sensational reporting, and consider the wider implications for societal perceptions and policy discussions related to these rare but highly publicized events.
